exemplar.title: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

strengths: Focus on the prescribed title and formulation of a clear central thesis (âknowledge shifts rather than destroys wonderâ).
Selection of two relevant Areas of Knowledge (natural sciences and the arts) and incorporation of pertinent TOK voices (AnaÃ¯s Nin, Nietzsche).
Evidence of counterclaims and alternative perspectives, showing an attempt at balanced reasoning.
Logical essay structure with an introduction, distinct AOK sections, and a concluding paragraph.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive; the link between examples and the knowledgeâwonder relationship is not consistently developed.
TOK concepts (perspective, interpretation, emotion, imagination) are mentioned but not explored in depth or tied systematically to examples.
Evaluation of opposing views is superficial; underlying assumptions and implications are not critically examined.
Transitions and cohesion are weak; the conclusion reiterates rather than synthesizes the argument and its implications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The overall performance sits squarely at a low Band-3 level (4/10) across all the gradable criteria: understanding of the title, analytical development, engagement with perspectives, and coherence of structure. Although the student demonstrates a coherent thesis and identifies relevant AOKs and counterclaims, their analysis is primarily descriptive, TOK concepts are under-developed, and critical evaluation of implications is limited. The structure is serviceable but marred by abrupt transitions and a concluding paragraph that lacks synthesis. To reflect the uniform weakness across all aspects of the essay while respecting the official total mark of 4, we allocate an equal share of 1 mark to each of the four gradable criteria (Understanding, Analysis, Perspectives, Structure), summing to 4.

annotations.comment: The Greek mythology reference is interesting but its relevance to the knowledgeâwonder relationship is implied rather than critically examined; probe the underlying assumptions.
Early reference to the natural sciences and the arts shows structural planning, but the rationale for selecting these two Areas of Knowledge remains underdeveloped.
The inclusion of AnaÃ¯s Nin and Nietzsche at the outset engages the reader and sets up contrasting perspectivesâthis effectively frames the debate and demonstrates awareness of relevant TOK voices.
The essay lacks an explicit formulation of a knowledge question in TOK terminology; defining the precise question would clarify the focus and strengthen the subsequent analysis.
The definition of âwonderâ is clear but overly simplistic; linking it to specific ways of knowing and knowledge frameworks would provide a stronger TOK conceptual grounding.
The thesis that acquiring knowledge âshiftsâ rather than destroys wonder is clearly stated; this provides a coherent proposition to develop throughout the essay.
Transitions between introductory ideas are abrupt; improving cohesion through clearer signposting would enhance the essayâs logical flow.
The description of the natural sciences aiming for definite answers introduces AOK characteristics but lacks connection to TOK concepts such as certainty and methodology.
The rainbow example remains largely descriptive; the analysis should explain how the scientific explanation (refraction) explicitly alters the sense of wonder.
The brief mention of implications (underappreciation of scientific discovery) identifies a consequence but does not explore its significance for our understanding of knowledge.
Mentioning âperspectiveâ recognizes ways of knowing, yet the essay fails to examine how sense perception or reason mediate wonder in scientific inquiry.
The natural sciences section merges multiple examples without clear sub-arguments; delineating distinct claims and supporting them sequentially would improve coherence.
While the biological details of pregnancy are correct, the essay does not critically analyze how this example deepens or diminishes wonder in relation to knowledge acquisition.
The claim that wonder is ârooted in our perspectivesâ is too general; distinguishing between personal viewpoints and shared knowledge frameworks would strengthen the analysis.
A counterclaim that medical knowledge can desensitize us to biological marvels is absent; including this would demonstrate balanced reasoning.
The arts section introduction notes that interpretation affects wonder but does not tie this to specific TOK ideas such as emotion or imagination.
Incorporating the Wilde quote adds literary flair, but the essay should assess its applicability and reliability within a TOK critique of art and wonder.
The counterpoint that intellectual understanding âkillsâ wonder is introduced but not critically examined; interrogate the underlying assumption and alternative viewpoints.
The analysis of Black Square remains surface-level; evaluate how different interpretations or cultural contexts alter the viewerâs sense of wonder.
The Taj Mahal example effectively shifts focus to historical context, yet the essay does not explore how cultural knowledge systems shape sustained wonder.
References to historical facts about the Taj Mahal are informative, but the essay should critically reflect on the reliability and perspective of these sources.
The final paragraph reiterates perspective shift but stops short of a decisive conclusion; a clear summary of main claims and their implications for the knowledge question would strengthen closure.
Sentence structure is confusing (e.g., 'wonder may not be fully destroyed by wonder'); careful editing is needed to clarify ideas and avoid circular phrasing.
The Works Cited demonstrates breadth but should follow a consistent citation style (e.g., MLA or APA) to ensure academic rigor and clarity.
The essay relies heavily on web sources; critically evaluate source credibility and consider including peer-reviewed or academic publications for stronger evidence.
Descriptive emphasis on beauty and emotion requires a connection to TOK frameworks (e.g., shared vs personal knowledge) to strengthen the argument.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
